segments.s372-g65: 'شه خسروان گفت' is a nominative+verb phrase: 'the king of kings said.' 'شه' (king) and 'خسروان' (kings) combine idiomatically as 'king of kings.' 'گفت' is the past tense reporting verb. The following words form a vocative address.
segments.s372-g66: This is a direct question: 'چرا' = 'why'; 'کردی' is the simple past 2nd-person singular ('you did/made'), so literally 'why did you make...'; 'این دیدگان' = 'these eyes' (demonstrative + plural noun); 'پر ز آب' is an adjectival phrase 'full of water', where 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' ('of/from'). The verb 'کردی' governs the whole object phrase 'این دیدگان پر ز آب.'
segments.s372-g67: 'کیان زاده گفت' names the speaker: 'Kiān-zādeh said' (کیان = the royal house / kings, زاده = born/son of). 'ای جهانگیر شاه' is the vocative address: 'O Jahangir, O king' (ای used as 'O!'). Word order places the address after the reporting verb, common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s372-g68: 'نبینی' here functions as a negative interrogative 'do you not see' (literal root دیدن 'to see'); 'که' introduces the subordinate clause 'that...'; 'بابم' = 'my Bab' (باب is a personal name here, so not covered in the provided noun senses); 'شد اکنون تباه' = 'has become now ruined' — 'شد' is the past of 'become', 'اکنون' 'now', 'تباه' 'ruined'.
segments.s372-g69: 'پس آنگاه گفت' is a temporal/discourse sequence: 'then/afterwards he said.' The vocative 'ای جهانگیر شاه' again addresses Jahangir, with 'ای' as 'O' and 'شاه' reinforcing his royal title.
segments.s372-g70: 'برو' is the imperative 'go.' The noun phrase 'کینه باب من' literally 'the grudge/vengeance of my Bab' (کینه used metonymically for 'revenge/vengeance'); 'بازخواه' is a terse, archaic/fixed-form verb meaning 'restore/bring back' or 'will retrieve' — here functioning as the action commanded (go and restore/avenge). The construction places the imperative first, then the object and the short verb 'بازخواه' in a poetic clipped order.
segments.s372-g71: 'بماندست' is an archaic/past-form meaning 'remained' or 'was left' (3rd-person past), so 'بماندست بابم' = 'my Bab remained' (بابم = 'my Bab'); 'بران خاک خشک' is a locative phrase 'upon that dry earth' ('بران' = contracted/poetic 'بر آن' = 'on that', followed by 'خاک خشک' = 'dry soil'). Word order places verb first for poetic effect.
segments.s372-g72: 'سیه ریش او' = 'his black beard' (سیه = black/dark; ریش = beard; او = he/his). 'پروریده' is a past participle 'nurtured, grown, tended' used attributively, and 'به مشک' is the instrument/means 'with musk' (به = with/into/by, مشک = musk), so the phrase describes his beard as being cultivated/perfumed with musk.
segments.s372-g17: "طلب کرد" is 3rd person past (he asked/requested). The verb comes first and is followed by the source introduced by "از" (from/of). "اسپ‌دار پدر" is a compound apposition meaning "the horse-keeper, (his) father" (پدر qualifies/identifies اسپ‌دار here).
segments.s372-g18: "نهاد" is the past of نهادن (placed/put). The following prepositional phrase "از بر او" (poetic ordering of از + بر + pronoun) indicates location/target ('onto him' / 'upon him'). "یکی زین زر" is a quantified noun phrase: "یکی" (= one) modifies "زین" (saddle) with "زر" in genitive/attributive position giving "a saddle of gold" (golden saddle).
segments.s372-g19: "بیاراست" is 3rd person past of آراستن (he adorned/ornamented). It is coordinated with the following noun phrase by the conjunction "و". The nouns "برگستوران برفگند" follow the verb as the objects/epithets being applied (in this poetic context they read as names/epithets or decorative terms associated with the thing adorned).
segments.s372-g20: "به فتراک" marks the place/thing affected ('onto/at the saddle-cloth'); "بر بست" is a compound sense: "بست" (past of بستن, to bind/fasten) with the preposition "بر" (on/upon) — 'he fastened on'. "پیچان کمند" is a noun phrase in which "پیچان" (winding/coiling) modifies "کمند" (lasso), yielding 'a coiling lariat'. Word order places the directional/locative prepositional phrase before the verb and the adjective before the noun.
segments.s372-g21: "بپوشید" (past of پوشیدن) is transitive: 'he put on / he clothed (someone)'. The object "جوشن" (cuirass/armor) follows the verb, and "بدو" ('to him') indicates the recipient (he put the armor on him). "بر نشست" is 'sat upon/mounted' — "بر" (on) + "نشست" (past of نشستن, to sit), so the sequence means he took his seat on (i.e. mounted).
segments.s372-g22: "ز" (poetic form of از) marks source/origin ('from'); "پنهان" functions as a noun/adverbial 'concealment/hidden place'. "خرامید" is past of خرامیدن (he strode/pranced proudly). The phrase "نیزه به دست" is an instrumental/adjunct: "نیزه" (spear) with the prepositional phrase "به دست" ('in hand', literally 'to/with hand') specifying that he held the spear.
segments.s372-g23: "ازین سان" (poetic compound of از + این + سان) means 'in this manner/thus'. "خرامید" is past tense 'he strode'. "تا" here marks the endpoint/destination ('to/until'), followed by "رزمگاه" (battlefield) as the destination. The clause therefore yields 'thus he strode to the battlefield.'
segments.s372-g24: "سوی" is the preposition 'toward(s)'. "باب کشته" is a noun phrase where "باب" (gate) is modified post‑nominally by the participle/adjective "کشته" ('slain') — 'the Gate of the Slain'. "بپیمود" (past of پیمودن) is transitive 'he traversed/covered', and "راه" (road) is the object: 'he traversed the road toward the Gate of the Slain.'
segments.s372-g49: 'بران' (prepositional/enclitic form) here points back to 'those [things]' — 'upon/for them'; it precedes the noun phrase. 'رنج و سختی' is a coordinated object ('suffering and hardship'). 'بپروردیم' is past tense, 1st person plural ('we nurtured/raised'), with the classical prefix b-; the verb takes the preceding phrase as the domain/condition of upbringing: 'we raised (him) on those sufferings and hardships.'
segments.s372-g50: 'کنون' is a temporal adverb 'now'. 'چون برفتی' is a subordinate temporal clause 'when/as you went' with 'چون' introducing the clause and 'برفتی' second-person singular past ('you went/left'). 'بکه' is a poetic/contracted form (literally 'بـه که' = 'to whom') and forms an indirect-object phrase with the following verb. 'اسپردیم' is past 1st-person plural 'we entrusted/gave over' — together: 'now, since/when you have gone, to whom have we entrusted (him)?'
segments.s372-g51: The cluster 'ت' + 'را' functions as the object pronoun 'ترا' ('to you'); the poet has split it into suffix and object marker. 'تا' here is an extent/limiting particle ('even/up to' or 'as far as') introducing what was given — 'سپه' (army). 'داد' is past 3rd person singular 'gave'. The order is typical epic Persian: indirect object (ترا/ترا تا سپه) precedes the verb.
segments.s372-g52: The conjunction 'و' links the next clause. 'گشتاسپ را' with 'را' marks the beneficiary/indirect object ('to Gushtasp'). 'داد' again is past 'gave'; 'تخت و کلاه' are the direct objects 'throne and crown' (what was given). In Persian word order the recipient is often fronted with 'را' while the thing given follows the verb.
segments.s372-g53: 'همی' is an iterative/adverbial particle giving a habitual or continuous sense ('ever/constantly'). 'لشکر و کشور' is a coordinated direct object 'army and realm'. 'آراستی' is a past (2nd-person) verb 'you arrayed/arranged/embellished' (in epic style the past + همی yields a habitual descriptive sense): 'you used to array/adorn the army and country.'
segments.s372-g54: Again 'همی' gives an ongoing/habitual colouring. 'رزم را' ('the battle') is marked by 'را' as the direct object. 'به آرزو' is an adverbial phrase 'with desire' (literally 'for/with longing'). 'خواستی' is past 2nd person singular 'you wished/wanted/sought' — together: 'you constantly desired/sought battle.'
segments.s372-g55: 'کنون' = 'now' sets the time. 'کت' ('coat' / cloak) is the grammatical subject (poetically foregrounded). 'به گیتی' = 'in the world' (prepositional phrase). 'برافروخت' is past 3rd singular 'kindled' or 'set ablaze' and takes 'نام' ('name, fame') as its effect — 'has kindled fame in the world.'
segments.s372-g56: 'شدی' is past 2nd person singular of 'شدن' ('you became' / 'you were'). 'کشته' is a predicate adjective/participial past 'slain'. 'نارسیده' (literally 'not arrived/ripe') qualifies the next phrase 'به کام' ('to fulfillment/satisfaction'); together 'نارسیده به کام' means 'before your desire was fulfilled' or 'unsatisfied.' The coordination 'کشته و نارسیده به کام' therefore: 'you were slain, (and) unfulfilled of your desire.'
segments.s372-g1: چو is the classical conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. اسفندیار is the proper name (subject). آن گو تهمتن is an appositive: آن ('that') + گو used adjectivally as 'so‑called/named' linking to تهمتن (the epithet Tahmatan), so the phrase means 'that one called Tahmatan.' Word order follows older Persian: temporal conj. + name + identifying apposition.
segments.s372-g2: خداوند اورنگ is a compound/title: خداوند ('lord, sovereign') governing اورنگ ('throne'), i.e. 'sovereign of the throne.' با + (سهم) + و + تن is an instrumental/attendant phrase: با 'with' marks accompaniment or instrument (here 'with spear'), و is the coordinating 'and', and تن ('body') means 'in person' or 'himself'—so the whole phrase says he came 'with spear and personally.'
segments.s372-g3: ازان کوه is an adverbial phrase 'from that mountain' (ازان = archaic از آن). بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' (third person). بانگ پدر is the object 'the father's cry' (noun + possessor). The clause order is adverbial + verb + object, which is common in classical Persian.
segments.s372-g4: به زاری is an adverbial phrase 'in lamentation' (به used adverbially). به پیش اندر functions together to mark direction/position 'forward/in front' (به = toward/into, پیش = before), and افگند سر is a compound verb phrase 'cast/let fall (his) head' (افگند past of افکند). The result: 'he cast his head forward in lamentation.'
segments.s372-g5: خرامیده is a perfective participle/past form meaning 'strutted' or 'strode'—here describing his entering action. نیزه به چنگ is an instrumental phrase 'spear in hand' (به used instrumentally), where چنگ here denotes the grip/hand. اندرون marks the location 'within/inward'—together: 'he strode in, spear in hand.'
segments.s372-g6: ز پیش پدر is a phrase 'before/in front of his father' (ز = from/of, پیش = before, followed by the possessor پدر). سر فگنده نگون is a resultative description: فگنده (past participle of افکند) 'having thrown' or 'cast', سر 'head', and نگون 'downward/overthrown' — together 'with his head cast down (before his father).' Word order places the locative phrase before the predicative clause.
segments.s372-g7: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (here 'a man' implicitly). دیزه‌ای is 'a rampart/fortification' (the -ای ending marks indefiniteness). بر نشسته is a verbal adjective phrase 'sitting upon' (بر = on/upon, نشسته = seated), and بلند modifies the place as 'high'—so 'a certain one sat on a high rampart.'
segments.s372-g8: بسان is a simile marker 'like/as'. یکی دیو is 'a certain demon' (used figuratively). جسته is the perfective/participle 'having leapt' or 'sprung', and ز بند means 'from bonds/fetter' (ز = from, بند = fetter). The clause reads 'like a demon sprung from its fetters.'
segments.s372-g25: همی before the verb marks iterative/continuous action (classical narrative progressive). تاخت is a past tense verb meaning 'galloped/charged'. آن باره is a temporal phrase 'at that moment/occasion' placed after the verb (classical flexibility of word order). تیزگرد here follows the verb as the subject/agent (subject placed after the verb is common in poetic Persian) and reads as an epithet/name/adjective meaning 'swift'.
segments.s372-g26: A series of short clauses: همی again signals continued action. آخت is an archaic/poetic verb used with کینه to mean 'was kindled/was inflamed' (here it describes the sudden rise of hatred). کینه is the nominal theme 'hatred/grudge'. The final portion همی کشت مرد uses کشت (past 'killed') with مرد as its patient; the omitted pronoun/subject ('he') is understood from prior clause — overall: 'hatred flared and he killed men.'
segments.s372-g27: از آزادگان 'from among the free (nobles)' — از marks the group. هرک is an indefinite relative 'whoever/whichever' and دیدی is 2nd-person past 'you saw/met' (here 'whoever you met'). به راه is a prepositional phrase 'on the road' (به + راه). Word order is flexible: the relative clause ends with the location.
segments.s372-g28: بپرسیدی is 2nd-person past 'you asked' (ب‑ prefix in classical narrative with past form). از introduces the object of inquiry 'about'. نامدار سپاه is a noun phrase 'the famed/renowned (figure) of the host' — نامدار qualifies سپاه; together they indicate whom you inquired about.
segments.s372-g29: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. اوفتادست is a past/perfect form 'fell/befell' (اوفتاد + ­ست). گفتی here functions as 'you said/you asked' and links the reported question to the addressee. زریر is the name of the person asked about; the clause is essentially the reported question 'Where did Zarrir fall?'.
segments.s372-g30: This is an appositive/epithet describing Zarrir: پدر 'father' + آن 'that' (demonstrative) + نبرده used adjectivally as 'not-taken/unconquered' (classical poetic epithet) + سوار 'horseman/rider' + دلیر 'brave'. The chain reads 'the father of that unconquered, valiant rider' (neb: نبرده here is a poetic adjective formed from the negative + برده).
segments.s372-g31: یکی 'one/a certain' introduces an indefinite person. مرد بد نام is a noun + adjective phrase 'a man of bad name' = 'ill‑reputed man' (in classical order the adjective or adjective phrase may follow the noun). او 'he' resumes the referent, and اردشیر is the proper name placed after the pronoun for emphasis ('he, Ardashir').
segments.s372-g32: This is a further descriptive tag: سواری 'a rider/horseman' modified by گرانمایه 'esteemed/precious' (a high-status epithet). گردی is a short poetic element here (contextually functioning as a minor epithet/ornament; in the supplied senses it corresponds to 'wandering' but in poetry can be part of a circumstantial descriptor), and دلیر is the adjective 'brave/valiant'. Together: 'an esteemed, valiant horseman.'
segments.s372-g9: بدان here functions as a locative/demonstrative phrase meaning 'into that / thereupon' (formed from the preposition ب- + an old demonstrative). لشکر دشمن اندر فتاد is a normal SOV clause with لشکر دشمن 'the enemy host/army' as subject and فتاد 'fell' (past) as the verb; اندر marks the inward/location relation 'into/within'.
segments.s372-g10: چنان چون ... introduces a simile 'just as / like when'. در افتد is a present/general-tense verb 'falls' (افتد) with در marking the notion of 'into/on'; به here expresses the agent/instrument ('by' the wind) and گلبرگ باد is the image 'a petal (گلبرگ) [moved by] the wind (باد)'.
segments.s372-g11: همی is an emphatic/progressive adverb meaning 'kept on / continually'. کشت here denotes killing (object implied: 'them') and ازیشان 'of/from them' marks the victims; the clause then adds 'و سر می‌برید' where سر 'head' is the object and می‌برید (mi- present progressive + بریدن) means 'was cutting off'. The sequence conveys continuous, repeated slaughter.
segments.s372-g12: The clause Zِ construction 'ز بیمش' = 'from/of his fear' gives the cause. همی again adds immediacy/continuation; مرد here functions as the verb 'died' (classical homograph of the noun) and stands before the relative expression هرکش بدید 'whoever saw him'. The whole line means 'from his terror, whoever saw him would die.'
segments.s372-g13: چو here means 'when'. بستور is the proper name (Bostur) and پور زریر is a patronymic 'son of Zarir' (پور = 'son of'). سوار is an appositive 'rider/horseman'. Word order places the name and lineage together before the predicate.
segments.s372-g14: ز خیمه 'from the tent' uses the preposition ز for 'from/out of'. خرامید is the past tense verb 'he strode / swaggered'. زی اسپ‌دار indicates direction 'toward the equerry/groom' (زی is an archaic directional/relational preposition here); اسپ‌دار names the addressee/target 'the groom / horse-master'.
segments.s372-g15: یکی here is the indefinite 'one/a certain'. اسپ آسوده تیزرو places two adjectives after the noun (typical Persian order): اسپ 'horse', آسوده 'calm/at ease', تیزرو 'swift'. Together they describe one steed as calm yet fast.
segments.s372-g16: This clause lists the other horses: جهنده 'leaping' describes one (جهنده یکی بود = 'one was a leaper'), with بود as the copula 'was'. آگنده خو is a compound adjective 'filled with sleep' (آگنده = full/filled, خو = sleep), describing another horse as drowsy. The structure enumerates distinct qualities of several horses.
segments.s372-g73: چو/از here (چواز) is the archaic conjunction 'when / since' introducing a temporal clause. پور 'son' is the source/agent in 'from the son' (چواز پور). بشنید is the past-tense verb 'heard' (archaic/formalized form of شنید); شاه is the subject (king) following the verb in the classical VSO order. این سخن is the object 'this speech' referring back to the preceding words.
segments.s372-g74: سیاهش is سیاه + -ش, literally 'his blackness / his dark countenance.' ببد is an archaic past/copular form (ب + بد/بود) meaning 'became/was.' روز روشن is a noun + adjective 'the bright/clear day.' ز بن (ز = poetic 'from') + بن 'root/foundation' is a prepositional phrase; here it is figurative ('from the root') modifying the change. The segment overall describes a dramatic change of appearance or mood.
segments.s372-g75: جهان is 'the world.' بر here marks relation/recipient ('for/to') — classical usage where English would use 'for' (the world became dark to the world-seeker). جهانجوی is a compound 'world-seeker' / 'ambitious person' (one who seeks worldly gain). تاریک شد is the predicate 'became dark' (شد = became). Note the verb follows the subject-phrase in this classical style.
segments.s372-g76: تن 'the body' is the subject. پیل واریش is a descriptive compound: پیل 'elephant' + واریش (from وار) meaning '-like' (elephant‑like). باریک شد is 'became thin/slender' (شد = became). The clause states that the great/elephantine body was reduced/grew thin.
segments.s372-g77: بیارید here is a classical/formal verb form meaning 'they brought (him in)' or an address to companions (contextual ambiguity); گفتا is the past 'said.' سیاه is used vocatively or as a name/epithet 'Siyah / the dark one.' م(را) and را are object markers/clitics—classical orthography sometimes separates them; the phrase indicates 'Siyah said (to) me' or 'Siyah said concerning me.'
segments.s372-g78: نبردی is the negative past-form (ن + بردی) meaning 'you did not take/carry (away)' addressed to a second person; قبا 'robe' and کلاه 'hat' are the direct objects linked by و 'and'. The final م/را sequence again marks the object 'my ...' or the definite object marker; overall the quoted speech says 'you did not take my robe and my hat.'
segments.s372-g79: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that/because'). امروز 'today' sets the time. من is the first-person subject 'I.' از پی is an idiom meaning 'in pursuit of / after' and governs کین اوی 'his vengeance' (کین = vengeance; اوی = 'his' in this classical pronominal form). The segment frames the speaker's motive: pursuing vengeance on his behalf.
segments.s372-g80: برانم is first-person present/subjunctive/optative of راندن ('I will drive/expel / I shall drive away'), here rendered as a future/intent. از + ین (ازین = 'from these') marks source/target group: دشمنان 'enemies.' خون 'blood' is the direct object; به جوی 'into the stream' is the directional phrase. Grammatically the verb governs the action ('I will drive the enemies' blood into the stream').
segments.s372-g57: شوم is an optative/1st‑person form ('may I be / let me go') used here to express the speaker's wish to go; زی is an archaic prepositional particle linking the verb to its goal ('to/for') and pairs with برادرت (برادر + the 2nd‑person suffix ت = 'your brother'). فرخنده شاه is a vocative epithet ('Fortunate King') following the name.
segments.s372-g58: فرود آی is a compound exhortation 'descend / come down' (فرود = descent, آی = 'come!'); گویمش is a 1st‑person verb with a 3rd‑person object enclitic (گویم + ‑ش) meaning 'I will tell him/it'. از خوب گاه is a prepositional phrase 'about/of the good time/occasion' (از = concerning; خوب = good; گاه = time/occasion).
segments.s372-g59: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('that/for that'); از تو here means 'because of you' (giving the cause); نه is the negative particle negating the predicate; این بد ('this evil/misfortune') is the logical subject; سزاوار اوی is a predicate meaning 'is fitting/deserved by him' with اوی the archaic 3rd‑person enclitic pronoun.
segments.s372-g60: برو is an imperative 'go.' کینش is 'his vengeance/vengeance on his behalf' (کین + possessive), followed by از دشمنان 'from/of the enemies' (از = from), and بازجوی is here a verb form 'seek/bring back' (imperative or verbal noun) meaning 'seek out' or 'recover' — the line urges going to obtain vengeance against the enemies.
segments.s372-g61: زمانی 'for a time/for a while' sets a temporal frame; برین سان is an older compound equivalent to بر این سان 'in this manner/so'; همی بود is the habitual/continuous past 'it was/it continued to be'; دیر here means 'for a long time' (duration).
segments.s372-g62: پس 'then/afterwards' introduces the next action; آن باره را is the definite object 'that occasion/matter' (اره = 'instance/affair' with the object marker را); اندر آورد is the verb phrase 'brought in/caused to be brought within' and زیر ('under') completes the idea 'brought under [his control]' — the phrase as a whole means he brought that affair under his sway.
segments.s372-g63: همی رفت is the past continuous/iterative 'he kept going' or 'he went on'; با بانگ 'with a cry' (با = with; بانگ = shout/cry) describes how he went; تا نزد شاه 'until to the presence of the king' (تا = until; نزد = near/in the presence of; شاه = king).
segments.s372-g64: که introduces a relative clause 'who'; بنشسته بود is a compound of the participle بنشسته (seated) plus بود (past auxiliary) meaning 'was seated'; از بر رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase combining از (from/of) + بر (on/by) + رزمگاه (battlefield) to indicate location 'by/at the battlefield.'
segments.s372-g89: This clause is a compact purposive phrase. 'به رزم اندر آید' literally 'into battle he comes' — the verb 'آید' (comes) is third-person and here used narratively (historic present). Word order is somewhat inverted (prepositional 'به رزم' + 'اندر آید'). 'به کین خواستن' uses the infinitive 'خواستن' with 'به' to express intent/purpose: 'to desire/seeking vengeance.'
segments.s372-g90: 'چرا باید این لشکر آراستن' is a rhetorical question. 'باید' is a modal verb meaning 'it should / must'; here it governs the infinitive 'آراستن' ('to array/equip'). The noun phrase 'این لشکر' is the logical object/subject of the action 'آراستن' (i.e. 'this army [be] arrayed/prepared').
segments.s372-g91: 'گرانمایه دستور گفتش به شاه' identifies the speaker and the addressee. 'گرانمایه' is an honorific adjective 'esteemed/precious' modifying 'دستور' (here functioning as a title/office). 'گفتش' is 'said to him' (گفت + enclitic ش = 'to him'), and 'به شاه' marks the indirect object 'to the king.'
segments.s372-g92: This is the reported speech: 'نبایدت رفتن بدان رزمگاه.' 'نباید' is the impersonal/negative modal 'must not'; the enclitic 'ـت' on نباید marks the second person ('you must not'). 'رفتن' is the infinitive 'to go.' 'بدان' is an archaic contraction of 'به' + demonstrative and means 'to that', and 'رزمگاه' is the destination 'battlefield.'
segments.s372-g93: 'به بستور ده باره برنشست' is an adverbial + verb clause. 'به' functions adverbially ('by/with' — 'in that manner') before 'بستور' ('thus, in this manner'). 'باره' ('occasion/instance') here with an omitted numeral forms the sense 'once more/again' (poetic 'ده باره' = repeatedly/again). 'برنشست' is a past narrative verb 'sat down' (archaic compound of بر + نشستن).
segments.s372-g94: This clause gives the action that follows: 'مر او را سوی رزم دشمن فرست.' 'مر' = 'to me' (enclitic-like old pronoun), 'او را' is the direct object 'him' (را marks definiteness), 'سوی' = 'toward,' 'رزم دشمن' = 'the enemy's battle/host,' and 'فرست' is the (narrative) past tense of 'فرستادن' 'sent.' Word order places the recipient 'مر' before the object for emphasis: 'he sent him to me toward the enemy's battle.'
segments.s372-g95: 'که او آورد باز کین پدر' is a subordinate clause introduced by 'که' ('that'). 'او آورد' is simple past 'he brought' and 'باز' modifies it as a separable adverb 'back/again.' 'کین پدر' is a genitive phrase 'the vengeance of (his) father.'
segments.s372-g96: 'ازان کش تو باز آوری خوب‌تر' expresses a comparative recommendation. 'ازان' = 'from/than that' (referring back to the previous clause), 'کش' functions here as an agentive noun element 'killer' (i.e. 'that killer'), 'تو آوری' is second-person present/subjunctive 'you bring' (with 'باز' = 'back' inserted), and 'خوب‌تر' is the comparative 'better' — the clause means 'it would be better if you brought back that killer.'
segments.s372-g81: یکی before a noun functions as an indefinite determinative 'a certain / one'; آتش is the direct object 'fire'; انگیزم is a 1st-person singular present/presentive verb 'I kindle/incite' (the -م ending marks the speaker). اندر جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world' following the verb, a normal classical Persian word order variation.
segments.s372-g82: کزانجا is a directional/adverbial 'from there/thence'; به marks the goal ('to/towards'); کیوان is the goal (the planet/heavenly body); رسد is 3rd-person singular present/impersonal 'reaches/arrives' (verb before its noun phrase is acceptable in classical Persian); دود آن 'that smoke' is the semantic subject/thing that reaches Kiwan.
segments.s372-g83: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; گردان is the subject 'the troop/contingent'; بدیدند is past tense 3rd-person plural 'they saw'; کز here functions as 'from which / that from' introducing the source phrase (i.e. 'from the battlefield').
segments.s372-g84: ازان is 'from that / from there' (pointing back to the source); تیره is an attributive adjective 'dark/ominous' modifying آوردگاه; آوردگاه (literally 'place of bringing') functions as 'gathering-place' or 'battlefield' here; سپاه is the head noun 'host/army'. The phrase forms a noun phrase 'that dark gathering-place of the host'.
segments.s372-g85: که introduces the subordinate/explanatory clause 'that/for.' خسرو here functions as 'the king' (a title used generically). بسیچید is a past tense verb meaning 'mustered/gathered' (often of troops). آراستن is an infinitive meaning 'to array/prepare/equip' and here expresses the purpose or object of the mustering (i.e., he gathered in order to equip/prepare).
segments.s372-g86: همی رفت is the iterative/continuous past 'kept going' or 'was proceeding.' خواهد + خواستن is an older-modal construction: خواهد (will/wishes) followed by the infinitive خواستن expresses intention or desire; به + کین + خواستن = 'to seek/take vengeance.' Word order is archaic/poetic but functions like 'he was going, intending to exact vengeance.'
segments.s372-g87: نباشیم is the negative subjunctive/hortative form of باشیم (با ن- prefix + باشیم), here functioning as quoted speech or a hortative formula meaning roughly “let us not be” or “we should not be.” گفتند is a perfective past 3rd-person-plural verb “they said.” همداستان is an adverbial/ adjectival phrase “of one mind / in unison” that modifies گفتند; together the segment reports what was said and that it was said unanimously. Word order places the quoted hortative first, then the reporting verb and its manner.
segments.s372-g88: که is the subordinating particle “that” introducing the content of their statement. شاهنشه (شاهنشاه) is the title “Shahanshah / emperor” serving as the grammatical subject of the subordinate clause. آن is a demonstrative/definite marker “that/the,” and کدخدای جهان is a nominal phrase “kadeh‑khodāy (village‑head / chief) of the world,” here used as a predicate nominal; the copula (“is/was”) is omitted (a common poetic ellipsis), so the clause means “that the Shahanshah is that head/leader of the world.”
segments.s372-g33: بپرسید is a past-tense narrative verb ('he asked'); it appears before its complements as is typical in older Persian. ازو is the prepositional phrase 'from him'. راه فرزند خرد is a compact noun phrase where the ezafe is omitted (poetic compression): راهِ فرزندِ خرد = 'the way of/the route to the (wise/young) son' or more naturally 'the way (asked of) the sensible youth'.
segments.s372-g34: سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)'; بابکش is the proper name 'Babak' (here the target/direction). راه بنمود is a verb phrase 'showed the way' (بنمود = showed/made known). گرد at the end is an archaic/poetic element signaling motion or completion (here functioning like 'went/turned' or an emphatic verbal particle), so the clause reads 'pointed/shown the way toward Babak.'
segments.s372-g35: فگندست is an archaic past-tense verb 'he threw' (the initial f- is part of the classical form); گفتا is another past-tense verb 'he said/uttered'—classical Persian often places two past verbs in sequence. میان سپاه is a prepositional/locative phrase 'in the middle of the army' (میان before the noun).
segments.s372-g36: به + نزدیکی = 'at the nearness of' = 'near'; آن درفش سیاه is a noun phrase 'that black banner/standard' with adjective following the noun, the ezafe omitted in poetic diction. The whole phrase gives a locative: 'near that black standard.'
segments.s372-g37: برو is the imperative 'go'; زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the command. کانجا is an archaic locative 'to/at that place' (there). فتادست is a past-tense verb 'fell' with the classical past ending -ست; اوی is the attached third-person pronoun 'he' (subject) appearing after the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s372-g38: مگر here introduces a conjecture/possibility (classical use 'perhaps' / 'unless' depending on context). باز is the adverb 'again'. بینیش is an archaic verbal form from دیدن ('to see') meaning roughly 'he may see' or 'he might behold' (formally a subjunctive/optative third-person); یک‌بار is 'once'; روی is 'face'. Together the phrase expresses a hopeful/uncertain possibility that he may once more see the face.
segments.s372-g39: پس is a connective adverb 'then/thereupon'. آن شاهزاده is the definite noun phrase 'that prince'. برانگیخت is a past transitive verb 'roused/raised' (here taking بور as its object), and بور (literally 'fair/pale') is a descriptive name for the horse ('the bay/fair horse') placed after the verb in classical poetic order.
segments.s372-g40: همی is the iterative/progressive particle (classical usage meaning 'was/kept on'); کشت here is used in the narrative sense of striking/slaying about (past of کشتن in epic contexts). گرد functions adverbially 'around/about'. و connects clauses; همی کرد is another compounded action 'he did/made', with شور the object meaning 'tumult/commotion'. Overall: 'he was slaying/about and he made tumult.'
segments.s372-g41: "بدان تاختن تا بر او رسید" is an adverbial + temporal/result clause. "بدان تاختن" uses the prepositional/locative particle (بدان = to/at that) followed by the infinitive تاختن (to charge) to mean "charging at that place/target"; "تا" here introduces the limit "until" and is followed by the past verb رسید (reached). "بر او" is the prepositional phrase "toward him / at him"; the subject is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s372-g42: "چو او را بدان خاک کشته بدید" is a temporal clause introduced by چو = "when". او را is the marked object "him" (ra marks definiteness/object); بدَن خاک here means "on that earth/ground" and کشته is the past participle "killed" modifying او را; بدید is the simple past "saw." Word order places the object phrase and its participial predicate before the matrix verb بدید (common in classical Persian).
segments.s372-g43: "بدیدش مر او را چو نزدیک شد" begins with بدیدش (بدید + clitic -ش = 'saw him/it'), followed by مر (an emphatic/dative form 'to me'/'for me' in classical usage) and an explicit object او را (repeated for emphasis). چو نزدیک شد is a subordinate temporal clause "when he came/drew near." The clause shows classical flexibility: clitic object, an emphatic/dative particle, and explicit object can co-occur before the verb.
segments.s372-g44: "جهان فروزانش تاریک شد" is a simple predication: جهان (the world) modified by فروزانش (فروزان + enclitic -ش = 'his/its shining') is the subject, and تاریک شد is the two-word predicate "became dark." The possessive enclitic -ش on فروزان (radiant) indicates possession ("his/its radiant world").
segments.s372-g45: برفتش is a past verb (برفت) with the enclitic third-person pronoun -ش, here meaning 'left him' or 'departed from him' (poetic past). دل و هوش are coordinated objects 'heart and consciousness/awareness' following the verb (typical poetic inversion). وز is the contracted poetic form of و + از ('and from') linking to the following phrase; پشت زین means 'the back of the saddle' (پشت + زین).
segments.s372-g46: فگند is an older past tense of افکند ('threw/cast') with an omitted or implied agent — here 'he/it threw' or 'he was cast.' از marks the source 'from/off.' برش should be read as بر + ش (a prepositional element with the enclitic -ش = 'off him/his'), not the noun 'برش' listed in the lexicon, so its provided senses don't match (see -1). خویشتن is an emphatic reflexive 'himself.' بر زمین = 'onto the ground' (بر + object).
segments.s372-g47: همی گفت is the iterative/continuous past sense 'kept saying' (همی marking continuous aspect; گفت here is the verb 'said' though the provided lexicon entry is nominal, so treat it as the verb). کای is the vocative particle 'O.' ماه تابان من = 'my shining moon,' with من after the noun functioning as a possessive ('my') in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s372-g48: This is a coordinated list with a final possessive: چراغ = 'lamp/light'; the sequence چراغ دل و دیده و جان + من means 'the light (or guide) of my heart, (of) my eye(s), and (of) my life,' where من at the end attaches possessively to the whole series (typical in Persian). دیده here means 'eye' (often used collectively for the eyes).

sentences.s372-s33: The king of kings said, "O dear Bab, why have you made these eyes full of water?"
sentences.s372-s34: Kiān-zādeh said, "O Jahangir Shah, do you not see that my Bab has now become ruined?"
sentences.s372-s35: Then he said, "O Jahangir Shah, go — avenge (or recover) the grievance for my Bab."
sentences.s372-s36: My Bab remained upon that dry earth; his black beard, nurtured with musk.
sentences.s372-s9: He asked his horse-keeper, the father; the father then placed upon him a golden saddle.
sentences.s372-s10: He adorned him (Bargesturān Barfagand) and fastened a coiling lariat to the saddle-cloth.
sentences.s372-s11: He clothed him in a cuirass and mounted; from concealment he strode forth, spear in hand.
sentences.s372-s12: Thus he strode to the battlefield and took the road toward the Gate of the Slain.
sentences.s372-s25: We reared him through those sufferings and hardships; now that you have gone, to whom shall we entrust him?
sentences.s372-s26: Lohrasp the king gave the command of the army to you, and gave Gushtasp the throne and the crown.
sentences.s372-s27: You ever adorned the army and the realm; you constantly sought battle with desire.
sentences.s372-s28: Now your coat has kindled a name in the world; you have been slain, unfulfilled of your desire.
sentences.s372-s1: When Esfandiyar — that so-called Tahmatan, sovereign of the throne — came in person with spear in hand.
sentences.s372-s2: From that mountain he heard his father's cry; he cast his head forward in lamentation.
sentences.s372-s3: He strode in, spear in hand; before his father he cast his head down.
sentences.s372-s4: A man sat atop a high rampart, like a demon sprung from its bonds.
sentences.s372-s13: Tizgerd charged swiftly at that moment; hatred flared, and he slew men.
sentences.s372-s14: You asked every free man you met on the road about the famed commander of the host.
sentences.s372-s15: You asked, 'Where did Zarrir fall?' — (he is) the father of that unbeaten, valiant rider.
sentences.s372-s16: One man, ill‑reputed — he was Ardashir — a distinguished, valiant horseman.
sentences.s372-s5: The enemy host fell into it, just as a petal falls when blown by the wind.
sentences.s372-s6: He kept killing them and cutting off heads; whoever saw him would die of fear.
sentences.s372-s7: When Bostur, son of Zarir, the horseman, strode out of the tent toward the equerry.
sentences.s372-s8: One horse was calm and swift; another was a leaper, and one was full of sleep.
sentences.s372-s37: When the king heard this speech from the son, his visage darkened as if the bright day were overturned from its root.
sentences.s372-s38: The world grew dark for the seeker of the world; the body, elephant-like, grew thin.
sentences.s372-s39: They brought him in; Siyah said to me, 'You did not take my robe and my hat.'
sentences.s372-s40: For today I, in pursuit of his vengeance, will drive the blood of these enemies into the stream.
sentences.s372-s29: May I go to your brother, O Fortunate King? Come down — I will tell him of the good occasion.
sentences.s372-s30: For it is not fitting that this ill should come upon him because of you; go, fetch his vengeance from the enemies.
sentences.s372-s31: For a while it remained thus; later he brought that matter under (his control).
sentences.s372-s32: He went on, crying aloud, until he came into the king's presence, who was seated by the battlefield.
sentences.s372-s45: If he enters the fight intent on vengeance, why should this army be arrayed?
sentences.s372-s46: The esteemed commander said to the king, 'You must not go to that battlefield.'
sentences.s372-s47: In that way he sat down once more and sent him toward the enemy's battle to me.
sentences.s372-s48: That he restored his father's vengeance — it would be better if you brought back that killer.
sentences.s372-s41: I will kindle a certain fire in the world, from which its smoke will thence reach Kiwan (Saturn).
sentences.s372-s42: When the troop saw that from the battlefield a dark host was coming.
sentences.s372-s43: For the king had mustered (his men) to make ready; he was going, intending to take vengeance.
sentences.s372-s44: They all said in unison, "Let us not be," — that the Shahanshah is the lord (headman) of the world.
sentences.s372-s17: He asked him the way; the sensible youth showed the route toward Babak.
sentences.s372-s18: He hurled (it) and cried out in the midst of the host, near that black standard.
sentences.s372-s19: Go quickly — there he fell, perhaps; may he again behold his face once more.
sentences.s372-s20: Then that prince roused his bay (horse); he rode about striking and made a tumult.
sentences.s372-s21: He charged at him until he reached him; when he saw him slain on that ground.
sentences.s372-s22: He saw him; when he drew near, his bright world grew dark.
sentences.s372-s23: His heart and awareness left him, and from off the saddle he was cast to the ground.
sentences.s372-s24: She kept saying, 'O my shining moon, lamp of my heart, my eyes, and my life.'
